issue_title: On MySQL backend, with the admin app enabled, if the admin view is in an atomic block, TransactionManagementError is raised.
issue_body:
For this particular case we have installed: 
* Django==1.10.6
* django-reversion==2.0.8
* django-import-export==0.5.1
* We use the django.contrib.admin app

In this particular project we are using these settings:
* ATOMIC_REQUESTS: True for all databases
* We have to add this middleware: reversion.middleware.RevisionMiddleware

When any admin view runs, these settings always run the view in an atomic block (in a transaction). Now, when importing a file with a Resource and an ImportExportModelAdmin in the admin app, we always get this error:
`TransactionManagementError: This is forbidden when an 'atomic' block is active.`.

This is because `import_export.resource.Resource.import_data()` tests for `connection.features.supports_transaction`. This is OK on most RDBMS, but on MySQL the support for transactions depends on the database engine being used, so Django runs a test to see if the DB supports transactions. During the test, a temporary table is created and also calls `set_autocommit(False)`. That call can only be executed outside of an `atomic` block (Django code test for `in_atomic_block` for the DB connection).

This also has the side effect the table created by Django (called ROLLBACK_TEST) to test transaction support is never deleted from the DB, because the rollback to the savepoint marked by Django is never performed.

I'll submit a Pull Request with a short patch to prevent this: if the `import_data()` is running inside an atomic block, then we know the DB supports transactions and we don't test for `connection.features.supports_transaction`.

issue_title: Markdown cell UX
issue_body: I like the cell automatically being in edit-mode on enter, in addition, I think we should render the markdown cell on exit, what do people think?

issue_title: Move desktop kernel's cleanup into epics (and out of reducers)
issue_body: While working on the nbextension I noticed probably the murkiest thing in the desktop app -- the restart, interrupt, and kill kernel logic all live inside reducers even though they're inherently asynchronous. The fix is pretty simple -- it's a bit more ritual around using epics for these, leaving room for us to do proper checking of the cleanup.

issue_title: [jext] directory listing view
issue_body: Similar to the classic notebook, we need a `/tree` directory listing for the jupyter extension. A component that already uses the contents API is in commuter -- we could put that in core and use them across the jupyter extension and commuter.

issue_title: flowconfig issue
issue_body:
It just dawned on me that our flow config is wrong.

```
module.name_mapper='^@nteract/(.*)$' -> '<PROJECT_ROOT>/packages/$1/src/'
```

will make `@nteract/play`, `@nteract/nbextension`, etc. all point to the wrong spot. I'm not sure if this effects things. Seems like a bit of funk though.

issue_title: Update descendant URL paths not implemented: moving pages & changing slugs broken
issue_body:
When **moving** a page or **changing the slug** of a page **with descendants**, all the descendant's `url_paths` must be updated to reflect the new slug (new slugs in every language to be precise) of the parent.

Currently the patched `Page.move()` method does this for the moved page. The call to `set_url_path()` (`wagtail_modeltranslation/models.py:450`) makes sure the `url_path` of the moved page is updated. However, if that page has descendants, those `url_paths` will be broken.

This is because `Page._update_descendant_url_paths()` (`wagtail/wagtailcore/models.py:506`) is not patched and in the original implementation is using raw sql and operates only on the `Page` table (and therefore the untranslated `url_path`), not the specific model which carries the translated slugs.

The same `Page._update_descendant_url_paths()` is called by `Page.save()` (currently *not* patched by modeltranslate) to propagare `slug`/`url_path` changes of parents to to their descendants. This means, that changing the slug of any parent page, breaks the children's `url_paths`.

issue_body:
Hi. I found that there is an if check in the elf.py
```
if (ph.p_vaddr - ph.p_offset) & (ph.p_align - 1) != 0:

```
You mentioned to see `https://code.woboq.org/userspace/glibc/elf/dl-load.c.html#1066
`
But I am still confused why you need this check and which specification of elf do this check map to? Many Thanks

issue_title: Handling for #base and #include
issue_body:
Apparently the VDF format supports definitions for merging files.

* `#base` will merge KVs from the specified file with the ones from the current
* `#include` will append

I don't see how that could be implemented in the module reliably. It should be fairly simple to implement at application level for the specific use case. That leaves allowing `#` symbol to be used as unquoted key or value, which is currently not supported.

issue_title: Generate graph edge costs based on measured performance
issue_body:
The `cost` for odo's graph isn't generated on the actual runtime cost of the conversion, which leads odo to [choose a conversion path that is sub-optimal](https://github.com/blaze/blaze/issues/1235).

To the extent that an edge's cost accurately reflects its actual runtime, the better odo can choose an optimal path from one format to another.

To generate these costs requires representative data sources for the different backends and a script that runs odo's basic converters for each backend pairing.  To be more accurate, the script would need to run on differently sized data and on different platforms, but even just a single platform run with decently sized data sources will improve the current heuristic costs.

These generated costs would be generated by the script and stored for usage at runtime.  Odo will need to be refactored to read in these generated costs and apply them to the graph edges.

@cpcloud thoughts?

issue_title: Vehicle.location returns wrong isRelative for copter
issue_body:
The location value has an isRelative field which is supposed to tell you if the altitude is relative to MSL or the home location. If it is true and you're sitting at the home location altitude should be zero. 

This is buggy for copter - if you look at the location value for (landed) Plane and Copter below you can see that the Copter value is broken - it should be True because we're sitting on the home position and altitude is zero.

* Copter: Location:lat=-35.3632507,lon=149.1652309,alt=0.0,is_relative=False
* Plane: Location:lat=-35.3632507,lon=149.1652309,alt=584.309997559,is_relative=False

issue_title: Calling a get/put/etc method with user=None is not equivalent to set_user=None
issue_body:
Hello!  I found a small inconsistency that’s mildly inconvenient.

Context: In a project where 90% of the views require authentication, I define a simple `app` fixture that calls `django_app.set_user('bill@example.ca')` and returns that testapp object.  For tests that check that the open views are indeed open, or that a specific view is indeed restricted (which is needed to make sure custom permissions work as intended with rest-framework for example), I can either use the base `django_app` fixture, or use my `app` and start the test with `app.set_user(None)` to remove `WEBTEST_USER` from the WSGI environment.

I found myself changing some tests to use a consistent style (pass `user=user2` or `user=None` everywhere instead of calling set_user in a few cases), and that did not reset the user.

Proposed fix: in `_update_environ`, use a sentinel object instead of None to determine if the `user` parameter was given or not; if it is None, remove `WEBTEST_USER`.

issue_title: Issue with SSL: required for all webhooks, but when it's plugged into Flask examples, it doesn't work
issue_body:
Hello,

Current Line API appears to require SSL for all webhooks (i.e. not just webhook URL verification). When I set up a working SSL context for Flask (see below), with a valid certificate chain from Let's Encrypt (root CA ISRG which is trusted by Line API), nothing appears to happen.

If I launch the examples (e.g. Flash echo bot) as is, I get HTTPS negotiation requests from Line API (Flask/Werkzeug complains as it's basically unexpected binary data, and can't handle it). However, if I set up a working HTTPS with a valid certificate (not self-signed), no webhook events are reported - I think Line API drops the connection since it is not satisfied with the HTTPS negotiation, but I'm yet to find a way for Werkzeug/whatever to debug-level report me all connection initiation attempts and how they look like:

```
        import ssl
        ctx = ssl.SSLContext(ssl.PROTOCOL_SSLv23)
        ctx.load_cert_chain('pb1.mkj.lt/cert.pem', 'pb1.mkj.lt/privkey.pem')
        app.run(debug=options.debug, host="0.0.0.0", port=int(options.port), ssl_context=ctx)
```

This runs fine, but no webhooks are reported from the Line API. The webhook URL verification on Line Manager also fails without further explanation.

How should this be done, and is there a way to debug SSL issues / get info from Line API what fails in particular?

Thank you!
Kostas

_P.S._ I've also just tried with `ssl.PROTOCOL_TLSv1_2` - same result.

issue_title: Setting Lbscripts and LHCbDirac versions
issue_body:
I have added some functionality to specify the version of LHCbDirac to use at runtime. It is just another LHCb config option. If left blank the default is used. It checks that the given version is valid by checking that the release folder exists - if it doesn't there is a warning and the default is used.

[`https://github.com/ganga-devs/ganga/blob/Lbversions/python/GangaLHCb/__init__.py`](https://github.com/ganga-devs/ganga/blob/Lbversions/python/GangaLHCb/__init__.py)

`configLHCb.addOption('diracVersion','',"set LHCbDirac version")`

`def _store_dirac_environment():
from GangaDirac.Lib.Utilities.DiracUtilities import write_env_cache, get_env
diracversion = _guess_version('LHCBDIRAC')
optionVersion = Ganga.Utility.Config.getConfig('LHCb')['diracVersion']
if os.path.isdir("/afs/cern.ch/lhcb/software/releases/LHCBDIRAC/LHCBDIRAC_"+optionVersion):
diracversion = optionVersion
elif optionVersion != '' :
logger.warning('LHCbDirac version %s does not exist, using default version %s instead',optionVersion,diracversion)
logger.info("Using LHCbDirac version %s", diracversion)
platform = os.environ['CMTOPT']
fdir = os.path.join(os.path.expanduser("~/.cache/Ganga/GangaLHCb"), platform)
fname = os.path.join(fdir, diracversion)
if not os.path.exists(fname) or not os.path.getsize(fname):
logger.info("Storing new LHCbDirac environment (%s:%s)" % (str(diracversion), str(platform)))
cmd = 'lb-run LHCBDIRAC {version} python -c "import os; print(dict(os.environ))"'.format(version=diracversion)
env = execute(cmd) # grab the stdout text
env = eval(env) # env is a string so convert it to a dict
write_env_cache(env, fname)
os.environ['GANGADIRACENVIRONMENT'] = fname`

issue_title: Ganga on non-case sensitive filesystems
issue_body:
Hi all,

I recently tried installing ```ganga``` on a well known OS which doesn't have case sensitivity turned on in it's filesystems by default.
(This was a quick exercise to get an estimate if we could support the platform).

As a result git and/or pip install becomes extremely confused about ```Ganga/__init__.py``` vs ```ganga/__init__.py```.

I know we're not aiming to support these environments but just before we go too far down the rabbit hole I'm curious to ask if we may want to reconsider the naming of ```ganga```?

I'm happy if we want to officially decide to not support these systems but I'm curious if there is an opinion as to whether it would be nice to run our testsuite elsewhere to help debug?

Thanks,

Rob

issue_title: XML Repo still seems a little fragile
issue_body:
Hi,

This is just a task to remind me to look into the XML repo code again in the near future as I keep coming across some situations where a job fails to load despite the backup file being perfectly readable. The expected behaviour is that it then loads the backup and sends a warning.

I don't think these errors occur during 'normal use' as I'm torture testing my repository with other tasks whenever I see this and I've not been able to reproduce it in a sane way yet.

I expect looking through the code should reveal that either an exception for missing file isn't handled somewhere or is mis-handled so it should be relatively trivial to track down by inspection.
/
Rob

issue_title: Relax record id validation
issue_body:
Do we really need to ensure that posted record ids match a uuid regex?

We can generate a uuid when a record without id is posted, and leave the usage of uuid in our official clients.
But is there any reason to use a different regex that collection and bucket names?

edit: The usecase is the Web sync extension chrome.storage.sync: since any key is accepted, it takes the md5 of the key to "generate" UUIDs. Instead we could let the client push any key as record id.
 
* Related #140

issue_title: revoke permissions on a record
issue_body:
Following the [First step tutorial](https://kinto.readthedocs.org/en/latest/tutorial.html#sync-and-share-data-between-users), I've managed to give Bob read access on Alice's task.

I've tried to figure out a way to revoke this permission, but I've failed to: it looks like PATCHing the record would only work by *adding* permissions, and not removing any of them.

Use cases:

* changing a "write" permission to a "read" one,
* revoking a permission for a specific user because she behave very badly.

```
$ echo '{"permissions": {"read": []} }' | http PATCH http://127.0.0.1:8888/v1/buckets/todo/collections/tasks/records/2b2fd6ad-5521-46d5-8712-5a27e42dd31f -v --auth 'alice:alicepassword'

PATCH /v1/buckets/todo/collections/tasks/records/2b2fd6ad-5521-46d5-8712-5a27e42dd31f HTTP/1.1
Accept: application/json
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate
Authorization: Basic YWxpY2U6YWxpY2VwYXNzd29yZA==
Connection: keep-alive
Content-Length: 31
Content-Type: application/json
Host: 127.0.0.1:8888
User-Agent: HTTPie/0.9.2

{
    "permissions": {
        "read": []
    }
}

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Access-Control-Expose-Headers: Backoff, Retry-After, Alert, Content-Length
Content-Length: 316
Content-Type: application/json; charset=UTF-8
Date: Tue, 04 Aug 2015 21:24:32 GMT
Etag: "1438722779308"
Last-Modified: Tue, 04 Aug 2015 21:12:59 GMT
Server: waitress

{
    "data": {
        "description": "Alice task",
        "id": "2b2fd6ad-5521-46d5-8712-5a27e42dd31f",
        "last_modified": 1438722779308,
        "status": "todo"
    },
    "permissions": {
        "read": [
            "basicauth:a103c2e714a04615783de8a03fef1c7fee221214387dd07993bb9aed1f2f2148"
        ],
        "write": [
            "basicauth:9be2b51de8544fbed4539382d0885f8643c0185c90fb23201d7bbe86d70b4a44"
        ]
    }
}
```

this one led to an error (it's expecting a list, for it's looping on it in cliquet):

```
echo '{"permissions": {"read": null} }' | http PATCH http://127.0.0.1:8888/v1/buckets/todo/collections/tasks/records/2b2fd6ad-5521-46d5-8712-5a27e42dd31f -v --auth 'alice:alicepassword'
```

1. is there a way to revoke a permission?
2. if it does exist, I think it should be mentioned in the documentation

issue_title: add more options (freq, ...) to NH_order_parameters
JET_CHANGE_FORM_SIBLING_LINKS not filtering the queryset based on preserved_filters
issue_body:
Guys,

I've found that the sibling navigation is currently ignoring preserved_filters when the _changelist_filters are present in the request.GET QueryDict.

apparently the try/except block at line 219 of jet/utils.py always raises a IncorrectLookupParameters exception saying **IncorrectLookupParameters: MODEL has no field named u'_changelist_filters**.

I managed to "solve" this issue with a workaround by monkey patching get_model_queryset and mutating the request.GET QueryDict before reaching the exception as follows:

```
if not request.GET._mutable:
    request.GET._mutable = True

request.GET = QueryDict(changelist_filters)
```
I know, probably not the best approach :/ But at least the thing is working for now.

It would be nice if you could take a look into this!

By the way, i'm using Django Jet version 1.0.6 / Django 1.9.7 / Python 2.7.2

Thanks
Sandro Salles

issue_body:

|---|---|
| Camera Model | SR300 | 
| Firmware Version | 2   | 
| Operating System & Version |  14.04  |
| Kernel Version (Linux Only)  |  4.4.0-97-generic |
Hi,
I could not install  librealsense2-utils from instruction guide. It showed the error:
The following packages have unmet dependencies:
 librealsense2-utils : Depends: libglfw3 (>= 3.1) but 3.0.4-2~trusty is to be installed
E: Unable to correct problems, you have held broken packages.

I tried to remove glfw3 but the problem still happens
Any help?
Thanks

issue_title: ngettext implementation is incompatible with standard ngettext
issue_body:
The classic ngettext interface is:

```
def ngettext(self, singular, plural, n):
```

Flaskext-babel extends this to be:

```
def ngettext(self, singular, plural, n, **variables):
```

in order to assist with doing the translation and interpolation steps in one go. Unfortunately applications relying on the standard interface can break, if they do the following:

```
str = ngettext('I like a car','I like %(cars)s cars',cars)
return str % dict(cars=cars)
```

In this case, if cars = 2, ngettext should return:

```
I like %(cars)s
```

Instead however the application crashes on https://github.com/mitsuhiko/flask-babel/blob/master/flaskext/babel.py#L479

```
    return t.ungettext(singular, plural, num) % variables
```

because flaskext is forcing an interpolation against variables, which is empty.

It is explicitly not the fault of callers expecting the normal behaviour, the standard behaviour of ngettext is well defined. Flaskext is breaking a number of dependent applications and libraries silently, including wtforms which makes use of this functionality in the Length validator.

issue_title: Executor Customization
issue_body:
I think that it would be beneficial to be able to pass a custom executor to a Graphene schema, so that it can use it to `execute` the query.


I'm thinking either:
```python
schema = Schema(query=QueryRootType, executor=Executor(...))
```
OR
```python
schema = Schema(query=QueryRootType)
schema.set_executor(Executor(...))
```

By default it should use the default executor instance. I am going to expose an API for this in `core.graphql.execution`

```python
from core.graphql import execution
default_executor = execution.get_default_executor()
```

The default executor will be configured to be a synchronous executor. In addition, you will be able to set the default executor using:

```python
execution.set_default_executor(Executor(...))
```

issue_title: What is the need for a specific Mutation class?
issue_body:
Hello all!

First of all, thank you for this library! It has been very useful for me.

Graphene's documentation on mutations (http://docs.graphene-python.org/en/latest/types/mutations/) says that `A Mutation is a special ObjectType that also defines an Input`. However query fields also define an input, but directly on the field definition.

By reading the guide in http://graphql.org/learn/queries/#mutations, mutations only really differentiate from queries in the fact that mutations are executed sequentially. So, my question is: why is there a Mutation class?

What would be the problem if I used the following?

```python
import graphene

class CreatePerson(graphene.ObjectType):
    ok = graphene.Boolean()
    person = graphene.Field(lambda: Person)

class MyMutations(graphene.ObjectType):
    create_person = graphene.Field(CreatePerson, args=dict(name=graphene.String()))

    def resolve_create_person(self, info, name):
        person = Person(name=name)
        ok = True
        return CreatePerson(person=person, ok=ok)
```

I believe this approach is more consistent with the paradigm used for creating queries, i.e., define CreatePerson as the object that is returned by a mutation that creates a person -- the mutation itself is implemented in `MyMutations` class (and there could be different functions for creating a person). 

Am I missing something?

issue_title: projector plugin's is_active method may cause TensorBoard to fail to load.
issue_body:
The projector plugin's is_active method may take a long time to resolve if there are many runs with checkpoint files. This could cause the data/plugin_listing endpoint to take a severely long time to resolve, which causes TensorBoard to be stuck at "Loading active dashboards ..." and eventually hit "Failed to load active dashboards".
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4221553/28767736-b54a50b8-7589-11e7-9e02-8a91303d0967.png)

We can tell that the projector plugin's is_active method is the culprit here by making the following code changes (to time is_active) and observing logs.

```
  @wrappers.Request.application
  def _serve_plugins_listing(self, request):
    """Serves an object mapping plugin name to whether it is enabled.

    Args:
      request: The werkzeug.Request object.

    Returns:
      A werkzeug.Response object.
    """
    response_object = {}
    for plugin in self._plugins:
      start = time.time()
      response_object[plugin.plugin_name] = plugin.is_active()
      time_elapsed = time.time() - start
      tf.logging.info(
          'is_active for %s took %d seconds', plugin.plugin_name, time_elapsed)

    return http_util.Respond(
        request,
        response_object,
        'application/json')
```

The logs seem to show that the projector plugin's is_active method takes a long time to finish:

```
I0727 22:46:52.563292  165659 application.py:209] is_active for audio took 0 seconds
I0727 22:46:52.563775  165659 application.py:209] is_active for debugger took 0 seconds
I0727 22:46:52.564146  165659 application.py:209] is_active for distributions took 0 seconds
I0727 22:46:52.564512  165659 application.py:209] is_active for graphs took 0 seconds
I0727 22:46:52.564885  165659 application.py:209] is_active for histograms took 0 seconds
I0727 22:46:52.565208  165659 application.py:209] is_active for images took 0 seconds
I0727 22:47:12.547991  165659 application.py:209] is_active for projector took 19 seconds
I0727 22:47:12.548487  165659 application.py:209] is_active for scalars took 0 seconds
I0727 22:47:13.093265  165659 application.py:209] is_active for text took 0 seconds
I0727 22:47:13.093442  165659 application.py:209] is_active for core took 0 seconds
```
 
An internal user with many subdirectories had experienced this issue. TensorBoard should be able to support as many subdirectories as possible, so this is a problem.

issue_title: [SED] Forwarding multicast messages
issue_body:
Currently when Router forwards MPL message to its SED children (destined to Realm-Local All Thread Nodes Address) it does it two times per one message, because MPL does retransmissions.

I don't see how current behaviour could violate Thread Specification, but what do you think about improving the process and forward such messages only once? Right now a second message will be discarded (due to MPL duplication) by SED anyway. Additionally, forwarding between Router and SED is ACKed on 15.4 layer, so its reliable.

If so, i have a simple fix by introducing a new subtype of Message object that indicates the next retransmission of MPL message, so MeshForwarder won't add message to the indirect queue.

issue_title: tflearn.utils.autoformat_stride_3d
issue_body:
[Docs](http://tflearn.org/layers/conv/#avg-pooling-3d) say that tflearn.layers.conv.avg_pool_3d should default to stride=[1,1,1,1,1].

Default for stride in the code is None.  No checking or default is done before sending None to tflearn .utils.autoformat_stride_3d.  This function handles ints or tuples/lists of ints, but not None type.

I think the following needs to be added before line 1267 in tflearn.layers.conv:
~~~~ 
if strides is None:
     strides = (1, 1, 1, 1, 1)
~~~~

It fixed my error at least.  May be needed in other pooling functions... (I didn't check)

issue_title: Fix test_which_editor_bad unit test on WSL
issue_body:
``cmd2`` runs fine on Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL), however there is one unit test failure in the **test_which_editor_bad** test.

This is because it is a test which is skipped on Windows and checks for the supposedly non-existent editor *notepad.exe*.  However, on WSL, that exists!  So this should be a very easy fix. 

One way is to add an extra conditional to this test to detect if this is running on a Linux OS in WSL using something like the following:
```Python
@pytest.mark.skipif(sys.platform.startswith('win'),
                    reason="utils.which function only used on Mac and Linux")
def test_which_editor_bad():
    import platform
    editor = 'notepad.exe'
    path = utils.which(editor)

    # Detect when running under Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL)
    if 'Microsoft' in platform.release():
        # This is a weird case where notepad.exe IS available on a Linux OS ...
        assert path is not None:
    else:
        # Assert that the editor wasn't found because no notepad.exe on non-Windows systems ;-)
        assert path is None
```

issue_title: Remove support for abbreviated commands
issue_body:
The ability to abbreviate commands has always seemed like an anti-feature.  The presence of good tab-completion of command names makes it completely unnecessary to allow for accepting abbreviated command names.  While accepting abbreviated command names can result in users accidentally doing something quite unintentional.  Supporting abbreviated commands significantly complicates other support for several other features and it has outright never worked in combination with multi line commands, which we have documented.

The ``cmd2.Cmd.abbrev`` attribute which supports abbreviated commands should be removed entirely.

Associated documentation, unit tests, and examples will all need to be updated and the CHANGELOG will need to adequately communicate this potentially breaking change.

issue_title: Console or dummy backend for testing
issue_body:
Feature request: Anymail provides several extensions to stock behavior such as setting `msg.anymail_status` after send,  and firing pre- and post-send signals, none of which happen when using  `django.core.mail.backends.console.EmailBackend`.  As a result, it's difficult to write integration tests for the behavior of signal handlers and status processing code.

A dummy or console backend which at a minimum provides `anymail_status` after send and fires off appropriate pre/post send hooks would make testing code that leverages those features much easier.

issue_title: implicitly_convertible doesn't allow specification of implicit conversion to basic data types
issue_body:
I have a a C++ class structure like this:

```
class A {
    ...
    void method(uint64_t val) { ... }
}
class B {
    ...
    uint64_t id() { ... } // returns unique identifier
    operator uint64_t() { return id(); }
}
```

i.e. where B is implicitly convertible to a uint64_t, with the intended usage to allow methods that only need the unique identifier to be called as:

`a_instance.method(b_instance);`

as a shortcut for:

`a_instance.method(b_instance.id());`

I tried to register this conversion using `pybind11::implicitly_convertible<B, uint64_t>()`, but the resulting module fails to import with:

`ImportError: implicitly_convertible: Unable to find type unsigned long`

Given that uint64_t is listed in the documentation's out-of-the-box "Supported data types" I rather expected this to work, but it does not.

I can work around this by defining two methods--one that takes B instances, and one that takes uint64_t values--but that seems a bit messy and inefficient for something that seems like it should work: i.e. my B instances *are* implicitly convertible to uin64_t, but I don't seem to be able to tell pybind11 about it.

(I did a quick test with std::vector and got the same error, so this doesn't appear to be something that only applies to primitive types.)

issue_title: Check unchecked types in certbot-dns-cloudxns
issue_labels: [{'id': 277080619, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20code%20health', 'name': 'area: code health', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 263357278, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20testing', 'name': 'area: testing', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 149596829, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/good%20first%20issue', 'name': 'good first issue', 'color': 'c61aff', 'default': True}, {'id': 281347824, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/has%20pr', 'name': 'has pr', 'color': '0052cc', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
This issue is for checking untyped definitions in the `certbot-dns-cloudxns` module.

To start, add the following to `mypy.ini` in the root of the repo:
```
[mypy-certbot_dns_cloudxns.*]
check_untyped_defs = True
```

Then run `tox -e mypy`. If passes, submit a PR. If not, fix errors and submit a PR when tests pass. You can read more about how all of this works below.

See explanation here: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1fp0tPP9bzYkUViTnOXAyoT_KfgTfs8HSpNMoU8Tm_SY/edit?usp=sharing

From http://blog.zulip.org/2016/10/13/static-types-in-python-oh-mypy/:

Add the `--check-untyped-defs` option to your mypy arguments, and get that running with no errors on the codebase. This option causes mypy to check every def in the codebase for internal consistency; mypy can detect many classes of bugs and mistakes in your codebase, without your having written a single type annotation! 

In most cases, you’ll want to fix bugs and bad code as you go, but you can also use #type: ignore annotations or exclude files to defer issues. For example, we excluded all of Zulip’s tests at first, since they’re both lower value to type-check and had most of the monkey-patching and other questionable Python in the project. For Zulip, getting clean `--check-untyped-defs` output took me about 2 days of hard work, including merging fixes for about 40 issues in the Zulip codebase. 

I spent another day or two generating nice reproducers for the mypy bugs I’d encountered and improving typeshed. Now that mypy is no longer in its infancy, mypy bugs are increasingly rare. But a large project should expect to encounter and fix typeshed issues (just submit a PR!). 

Once `mypy --check-untyped-defs` passes on your codebase, you should lock down your progress by running the mypy checker in your CI environment.

Because mypy type annotations are optional, once you’ve done the setup above, you can start annotating your codebase at whatever pace you like. Over time, you’ll get the benefits of static types in the parts of the codebase that you’ve annotated, without needing to do anything special to the rest of the codebase (the wonders of gradual typing!). In the next section, I’ll discuss strategy for how to move the codebase towards being fully annotated.

issue_body: Because of the import of symbols `from acme.magic_typing`, the nginx plugin released in 0.25 depends on acme 0.25 or better.  However, setup.py only lists `acme>0.21.1`, leading to a failure to build from source (and potential run-time failures).

issue_title: Deprecate --allow-subset-of-names
Add automatic ACME protocol detection mechanism to the acme library
issue_body:
This should work by querying the `directory` endpoint of the server and looking for new fields and/or the new names for existing fields to correctly determine the protocol version that should be used.

This should be done in a way where we store the value of the `directory` so `acme.client.Client` doesn't have to request it again. This should probably be accomplished by adding a new optional parameter to the ACME client which is the value of the directory.

There are many options on the return value here. The current plan is to have two different ACME client classes for each protocol (see #5287). With this in mind, the return value could be int/enum and directory value, ACME client type and directory, initialized ACME client of the right type, etc. I'm fine with any approach, but we have to live with the changes we make here for a long time so they should be designed carefully.

issue_title: Avoid depending on `db.eval` for shared Mongo environments
issue_body:
<!-- Provide a general summary of the issue in the Title above -->

## Detailed Description

As noted on #881, a shared Mongo environment (e.g. Heroku MongoLabs) will complain about not being authorized to run `db.eval` when running `udata init`. This is because `eval` requires admin privilege.

Also, `db.eval` is deprecated since 3.0 https://docs.mongodb.com/manual/reference/method/db.eval/.

## Context

As a user, I want to be able to install `udata` on a shared Mongo instance, for example on Heroku.

## Possible Implementation

Remove the dependency to `db.eval` (used for DB migrations). Investigate https://docs.mongodb.com/manual/reference/method/db.loadServerScripts/.

## Your Environment

`dev` branch, Heroku middlewares.

issue_title: Include partner network status/presence in channels endpoints
issue_body:
## Proposal

As we already have neighbors (nodes with which we have a channel with) availability/reachability in our state (through `ActionChangeNodeNetworkState` state changes), we should include it in the channel's API responses, so the client (e.g. webUI) can display a nice indicator when the partner of a channel is online or not.

Example:
```
http http://localhost:5001/api/1/channels
[
    {
        "balance": 100,
        "channel_identifier": "0x6b44e524596ada26184cbc177ca8b4b3fdc31fed8f75bc7faa65cec09e36784d",
        "partner_address": "0xE0902284c85A9A03dAA3B5ab032e238cc05CFF9a",
        "partner_status": "reachable",
        "reveal_timeout": 10,
        "settle_timeout": 500,
        "state": "open",
        "token_address": "0x0f114A1E9Db192502E7856309cc899952b3db1ED",
        "token_network_identifier": "0x7692624c0C43285D51c0DdDfb2A6388f5002762E"
    }
]
```

For the `unreachable` status, we may also include the _last seen_ information on it, which is simply the time of the last `NODE_NETWORK_REACHABLE` event for that partner:
```
        "partner_status": "unreachable since 1533045701",
```

issue_title: Assert loops in all contracts are sanely bound
issue_body:
This issue is also a subpart of #84 but I believe it's important enough to warrant its own ticket.

Unbound loops in solidity are very dangerous. At the moment we have a lot of `for()` loops in the contracts which are probably fine but we are bound by the block gas limit.

All for loops should be checked and proper checks be put in place, or the code should somehow be handled differently.

For example [here](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/smart_contracts/ChannelManagerLibrary.sol#L45) we are iterating all the open channels and perform actions for each one. Once the number of open channels makes that loop reach the block gas limit the contract will be blocked forever.

All of our loops should be addressed and either limits be put in place or the code should be rethought.

issue_title: Default expiry of 24h may lead to errors
issue_body:
In my experience, the default expiry of 24hours might be causing errors on the push backends of Google and Mozilla. They reject the request, with Mozilla reporting that the expiry is too far in the future, probably due to slight clock differences.

I would suggest to reduce the default expiry time in order to avoid such problems.

issue_title: Investigate use of `collections.deque` as containers for individuals in nodes
issue_body:
Currently lists are used but `deques` ("list-like container with fast appends and pops on either end") :https://docs.python.org/2/library/collections.html#collections.deque offer significant speed improvements for actions like `append` and `pop`.

This is due to the fact that lists have to copy and reindex each element during a `pop`.

I believe the only methods needed would be: `append` and `popleft` (we need to specify which type of `pop`) we want.

`deque`s are in the standard lib (in `collections`) and are in py2.4+

Example of the functionality that I believe we need:

```
>>> import collections
>>> queue = collections.deque()
>>> queue
deque([])
>>> for ind in [1, 4, 5]:
...    queue.append(ind)
>>> queue
deque([1, 4, 5])
>>> len(queue)
3
>>> queue.popleft()
1
>>> queue.popleft()
4
>>> len(queue), queue
1, deque([5])
```

We should see what this would do performance wise by using the timing benchmarks used in the paper.

issue_title: Conda installer should enforce Pillow 4.0.0.
issue_body: Installing PyTorch and torchvision via conda forces a downgrade from Python 3.6.1 to Python 3.6.0. But using Pillow 4.1.0 with Python 3.6.0 causes an error, as [Pillow 4.1.0 was built with Python 3.6.1](https://github.com/python-pillow/Pillow/issues/2479#issuecomment-292252147). The offending [bug is in Python](https://bugs.python.org/issue29943), not PyTorch, torchvision, or Pillow. However, the conda installer could be updated to require Pillow 4.0.0 if it requires Python 3.6.0.

issue_title: Fatal error in actor test.
issue_body:
I can't reproduce it reliably, but occasionally I can cause `testInheritActorFromClass` in `actor_test.py` to fail by running the following.

```
for i in {1..500}; do python test/actor_test.py ActorInheritance.testInheritActorFromClass; done
```

I have seen it fail with a check failure in line 504 in `local_scheduler.cc`, which is

```
CHECK(ActorID_equal(TaskSpec_actor_id(spec), NIL_ACTOR_ID));
```

Object reconstruction should be suppressed before it gets to this point. In this particular test, no objects should be evicted and no processes should be failing.

It is a little unclear what is happening here.

issue_title: Submitting a task with configurable num_return_vals should always return a list
issue_body:
### System information
- **OS Platform and Distribution (e.g., Linux Ubuntu 16.04)**: OSX 10.12
- **Ray installed from (source or binary)**: source
- **Ray version**: 0.5.0
- **Python version**: 2.7
- **Exact command to reproduce**: `my_task._submit(num_return_vals=1)`

### Describe the problem

If the `num_return_vals` parameter is *explicitly* passed into `task._submit()`, it should intuitively always return a list, even if the value passed in is 1. Currently it only returns a list of object IDs if `num_return_vals > 1`. This is because the user might be determining `num_return_vals` at runtime and shouldn't have to handle two cases separately.

### Description

I noticed that the "Players" page lists PotPlayer unable to handle stdin pipe mode. This is not true. PotPlayer does have a built-in pipe streaming source filter.

There is a trick though. I found that the stdin ( - ) can only be used as input if the working directory is user's home directory (`$USERPROFILE`).

Please update the page for this information.

Example PowerShell command to launch the URL from clipboard:

issue_body:
The code here:
https://github.com/django-helpdesk/django-helpdesk/blame/master/helpdesk/management/commands/get_email.py#L324

Seems to be expecting to receive escaped unicode. However in my environment that doesn't appear to be case. The committed code raises a UnicodeEncodeError when reading unicode emails.

My workaround:

```
                if six.PY3:
                    try:
                        # Original workaround to get unicode text out rather than escaped text
                        body = body.encode('ascii').decode('unicode_escape')
                    except UnicodeEncodeError:
                        pass # >>> Support receiving real unicode too...
                else:
                    body = body.encode('utf-8')
```

email-reply-parser (0.5.9)

issue_title: RTL attribute for all (most?) widgets

This is mostly a marker - I'm willing to try to take this on, but I want to record it if someone else has thoughts.

I have a bunch of meta-form-widgets that have basic RTL support in their global layout, but the primary widgets (dropdowns, text areas, etc.) don't support RTL so they're basically useless. issue_title: Spec clarity: Semantic versioning vs. simple incremented numbers
issue_body:
Reading the document 'Versioning.md,' one is generally left with the impression that ONNX expects version numbers to follow the conventions of semantic versioning, and the text describes best practices in some detail. 

For IR and model versions, it explicitly mentions semantic versioning conventions, but remains silent for operator sets. In fact, the 'prose' example of writing operator ids suggests that versions are simple monotonically increasing numbers:

``
A given operator is identified by a three-tuple: (domain, op_type, and op_version). This is written as domain.op_type:op_version in prose (e.g., com.acme.FastConv:3). Nodes in graphs always refer to operators by their three-part identifier.
``

Other content also suggests that operator sets use simple number increments, as does the Operators.md and Operators-ml.md files, which refer to operator set versions using a simple number scheme.

I note that using semver for IR versions means that we're now on 0.0.3. Based on the conventions for semantic versioning listed in Versioning.md, we should at least be on version 0.3, if not 3.0, depending on whether the changes we introduced were truly BC-breaking (I don't recall). My observation is that the IR versioning to date seems to effectively be using simple incrementing numbers rather than semantic versioning.

I propose that we recognize that the version numbers being integers easily facilitates supporting both semver and simple increasing numbers. We could add a rule that says that leaving the MAJOR/MINOR tuple (0,0) indicates simple numbers, anything else indicates semver.

I further propose that we recognize that the IR version scheme is, effectively, already simple incrementing numbers, and that we change the language in the specification to reflect that.

I also propose that the ONNX specification remain silent on whether models should use semantic versioning or increasing numbers, but that models are expected to follow the outlined conventions if they choose to use semver.

issue_title: Emit a signal if a user was successfully authenticated
issue_body: As far as I could read from the documentation, the only signal that gets emitted on the first successful authentication is the `populate_user` signal. What I would need is a signal on every successful authentication, not depending on any population of user data. My current workaround is to set `AUTH_LDAP_ALWAYS_UPDATE_USER` to `True` what triggers the `populate_user` signal on every successful authentication. If I don't want to populate the data on every authentication attempt, I currently don't see any way for getting notified (outside of the function that invokes the backend) if authentication succeeded. Do I miss something and if not, what do you think about implementing such a signal? Thank's again!

issue_title: require() returns single-element array
issue_body:
When I do something like `var c = require('config.yaml');` I get a single-element array with the YAML-derived data structure as the element. I guess that's more or less as documented, but it seems strange. I would have expected that I'd get just the data structure, with no extra array around it. Having to use a `[0]` to get to the actual data is an annoyance. Is there some point I'm missing?

That is, I'd hoped to get

    {"property1":"value1","property2":"value2"}

but instead I get

    [{"property1":"value1","property2":"value2"}]
